link_attribute_type_list.name: dolceola

link_attribute_type_list.description: The minipiano is a type of piano in which the sound producing mechanism is positioned below the keyboard, allowing an economical use of space.
link_attribute_type_list.name: minipiano

link_attribute_type_list.description: Ancient bowed fiddle, once popular in Western India and Sri Lanka.
link_attribute_type_list.name: ravanahatha

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of equusine jawbone, it is used in traditional Latin-american music as a rattle.
link_attribute_type_list.name: quijada

link_attribute_type_list.description: Important to the Garifuna culture, it consists of two drums, the tenor, primero and the bass, segunda, each is made of hollowed out wood with membranes of pig, sheep or deer.
link_attribute_type_list.name: garifuna drum

link_attribute_type_list.description: Bass member of the garifuna drum group.
link_attribute_type_list.name: segunda

link_attribute_type_list.description: Tenor member of the garifuna drum group.
link_attribute_type_list.name: primero

link_attribute_type_list.description: Small Polish traditional fiddle.
link_attribute_type_list.name: oktawka

link_attribute_type_list.description: Sopilka (generally) refers to a type of fife used in Ukrainian traditional music.
link_attribute_type_list.name: sopilka

link_attribute_type_list.description: The citole is an archaic musical instrument, similar and a distant ancestor of the modern guitar.
link_attribute_type_list.name: citole

link_attribute_type_list.description: The buisine was a type of straight medieval trumpet usually made of metal.
link_attribute_type_list.name: buisine

link_attribute_type_list.description: A guitar synthesizer is any one of a number of systems that allow a guitar player to play synthesizer sound.
link_attribute_type_list.name: guitar synthesizer

link_attribute_type_list.description: Known in Persia as far back as the 14th century, it consists of glass containers, tuned by either being filled with water or later being of different sizes. Played by rubbing dampened fingers on the rims.
link_attribute_type_list.name: glass harp

link_attribute_type_list.description: A mandolute is a North African fretted string instrument which combines a traditional oud string format with five pairs of metal strings
link_attribute_type_list.name: mandolute

link_attribute_type_list.description: MIDI controller with a marimba-layout inspired control deck
link_attribute_type_list.name: marimba lumina

link_attribute_type_list.description: Played in folk music of Hungary and Transylvanian regions, its 3-4 strings are plucked or struck with a stick, and its soundbox is made of thick maple, poplar or willow wood.
link_attribute_type_list.name: ütőgardon

link_attribute_type_list.description: Traditionally consisting of two long wooden planks with corn-husks attached for vibration and hit with long, thick sticks called Makilak, today the planks are shorter and can be made of many other materials like stone, wood or metal and are hit with shorter, lighter sticks.
link_attribute_type_list.name: txalaparta

link_attribute_type_list.description: This indicates an artist cancelled their appearance at an event.
link_attribute_type_list.name: cancelled

link_attribute_type_list.description: European mediaeval struck string instrument, similar to the psaltery and a possible ancestor of the piano. Basically it is a dulcimer with keys.
link_attribute_type_list.name: dulce melos

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made portable with a shoulder-strap like a guitar, electric keyboard has soundcontrols on the neck
instrument_aliases: ショルダーキーボード
link_attribute_type_list.name: keytar

link_attribute_type_list.description: electronic bass organ proto-keytar
link_attribute_type_list.name: tubon

link_attribute_type_list.description: Six-stringed lute-harp from Wassoulou, West Africa. Traditionally played by hunters, its resonator box is made of calabash covered by animal skin.
link_attribute_type_list.name: donso ngɔni

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of dried gourd. Struck with hands or objects.
link_attribute_type_list.name: calabash

link_attribute_type_list.description: Harmonica/Accordion hybrid where the bellows for the accordion bit with buttons/keys receive air though the user blowing into the instrument like an harmonica
link_attribute_type_list.name: accordina

link_attribute_type_list.description: Distinctly asymmetric in contrast to typical guitar, its timbre is a combination of the harpsichord (pt:cravo) and the Viola caipira. It can have six or twelve nylon or steel strings and are produced solely by company Giannini.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: guitar/Viola caipira like plucked string instrument
link_attribute_type_list.name: craviola

link_attribute_type_list.description: free reed melodica like instrument that is worn like an accordion, with differenting length pipes which control the pitch
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: free reed aerophone with pitching pipes.
link_attribute_type_list.name: claviola

link_attribute_type_list.description: Traditional musical instruments of the Māori people of New Zealand. Consists of gourds (hue), shell flutes (pu), wooden flutes (koauau), wind roarers and other natural materials.
link_attribute_type_list.name: taonga pūoro

link_attribute_type_list.description: cross-blown flute made of stone, bone or wood. It is 10 to 39 cm long, with usually 3, but everything from none to 5 holes is known.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro small cross-blown flute
link_attribute_type_list.name: kōauau

link_attribute_type_list.description: Small vessel flute made of wood, clay, bone or soft stone like soapstone, it has 4 holes and is played with the nose or mouth.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro small vessel flute
link_attribute_type_list.name: nguru

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of wood like mataī or tutu, this long flute has a transverse blowing hole with a fipple like European flutes but finger holes in the style of the traditional pōrutu.
link_attribute_type_list.name: rehu

link_attribute_type_list.description: Long wooden flute with a notched open top and a single finger hole near the end.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro long one-holed flute
link_attribute_type_list.name: pūmotomoto

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of wood and shaped after the casemoth cocoon, it can be played as both a flute (female voice) and a trumpet (male voice).
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro wooden dual-voiced flute
link_attribute_type_list.name: pūtōrino

link_attribute_type_list.description: A longer version of the kōauau, it is from 30-40 cm up towards 60 cm in length and often made of bone and hardwoods such as porokaiwhiria, mataī and tutu.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro two harmonic flute
link_attribute_type_list.name: pōrutu

link_attribute_type_list.description: Up to 2,5 meters long, this wooden trumpet was used for a variety of announcements like war, welcoming people and kumara planting.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro wooden announcement trumpet
link_attribute_type_list.name: pūkaea

link_attribute_type_list.description: Trumpet made of conch shell, it has a beautifully carved wooden mouthpiece. It was for signalling, but also ceremonial and ritual use.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro conch shell trumpet
link_attribute_type_list.name: pūtātara

link_attribute_type_list.description: Gong made of pounamu; greenstone and pilot-whale bone with a striker made of akeake, a hardwood.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro gong made of jade and bone
link_attribute_type_list.name: pahū pounamu

link_attribute_type_list.description: made of wood, bone or stone it has a long string attached, when spun around it produces a deep, loud whizzing sound that can be heard from far away.
link_attribute_type_list.name: pūrerehua

link_attribute_type_list.description: A hollowed out gourd (hue) with one to three holes, swung around creating a whistling, chattering sound that resembled birds
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro swung whistling gourd
link_attribute_type_list.name: poi āwhiowhio

link_attribute_type_list.description: An electromechanical piano (not to be confused with electronic piano) designed and produced by Hohner in the 1960 and 70s.
link_attribute_type_list.name: Pianet

link_attribute_type_list.description: electromechanical piano with stainless steel reeds and an amplified pick-up. not to be confused with "cembalo" which is another name for Harpsichord
link_attribute_type_list.name: Cembalet

link_attribute_type_list.description: small white electric thumb piano with metal tines and an electromagnetic pickup.
link_attribute_type_list.name: Guitaret

link_attribute_type_list.description: An effects device to be clamped on the bridge, it produces "synthesizer-like" sounds
link_attribute_type_list.name: gizmo

link_attribute_type_list.description: A smaller harpsichord, often with the strings at 30 degrees from the keyboard.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: A smaller harpsichord, strings at an angle
link_attribute_type_list.name: spinet

link_attribute_type_list.description: small piano with drop action stickers manufactured from around 1930 to the early 1990s.
link_attribute_type_list.name: piano spinet

link_attribute_type_list.description: Exceptionally long spinet, with multiple strings choirs, its bass-tones were produced by longer, not thicker strings.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: extralong spinet with deep bass register
link_attribute_type_list.name: spinettone

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chiefly Scandinavian, these bronze age natural brass horns were ritualistically buried in pairs.
link_attribute_type_list.name: bronze lur

link_attribute_type_list.description: Known from the Viking age, this natural brass trumpet/horn was made of wood or birch bark
link_attribute_type_list.name: birch lur

link_attribute_type_list.description: Round-backed lute ancestral to the guitar
link_attribute_type_list.name: gittern

link_attribute_type_list.description: Use this to indicate that the mastering relationship is specifically for a remaster.
link_attribute_type_list.name: re

link_attribute_type_list.description: a spanish plucked cittern, it is also played in the spanish diaspora.
link_attribute_type_list.name: laúd

link_attribute_type_list.description: A cross between a saxophone and a duduk invented and played by Alexander Berne
link_attribute_type_list.name: saduk

link_attribute_type_list.description: Ancient pipe originating in Europe, with analogies all over the world, it is often combined with tabor drums.
link_attribute_type_list.name: three-hole pipe

link_attribute_type_list.description: Side drum, often with one or more snares, worn by one person and beaten by one hand, it is often combined with tabor pipes.
link_attribute_type_list.name: tabor

link_attribute_type_list.description: Originally an early European combination of a drum (tambourine) and pipe (fipple flute) were pipe was played by one hand and drum beaten by the other. It was chiefly used for dancing music. It has analogies all over the world.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: pair of flute and drum, each played by one hand
link_attribute_type_list.name: pipe and tabor

link_attribute_type_list.description: Electric instrument from the 1930's with a resistor wire and moving metal plate.
link_attribute_type_list.name: trautonium

link_attribute_type_list.description: there are two types: temür/tömör khuur (steel) and khulsan khuur (bamboo)
link_attribute_type_list.name: aman khuur

link_attribute_type_list.description: used in folkmedisine and shamanistic rituals
link_attribute_type_list.name: temür khuur

link_attribute_type_list.description: used for folksongs, particularly by young girls.
link_attribute_type_list.name: khulsan khuur

link_attribute_type_list.description: conical goatskin frame-drum originating with the Yoruba people of West Africa, it is also found in Afro-Caribbean and Afro-Latin American cultures.
link_attribute_type_list.name: ashiko

link_attribute_type_list.description: using analogue circuits and techniques to produce sound electronically, the first types where created in the 1920's with thermionic valves and other electromechanical machineries.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: uses analogue circuits to produce sound
link_attribute_type_list.name: analog synthesizer

link_attribute_type_list.description: one of the first analogue synths, it had a filter bank with 15 sliders for different sounds, and a "vibrato" inducing keyboard based on the ondes Martenot's.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: early analogue synth with various sounds and "vibrato" keyboard
link_attribute_type_list.name: ondioline

link_attribute_type_list.description: an early analogue synthesizer, it had a vacuum tube oscillator and high/low pass filtering to produce vibrato
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: electronic keyboard, forerunner to analogue synthesizers
link_attribute_type_list.name: clavioline

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Improved further by Jaffar Khan, it had a wooden soundboard, the fingerboard covered with iron and all strings are steel or bronze.
Less common today, it was used for Dhrupad style music.
link_attribute_type_list.name: sursingar

link_attribute_type_list.description: Modern derivative of the donso ngɔni, it comes with more strings and is made of calabash. It became an important instrument in the rise of the Wassoulou music in the 80's and 90's.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Modern ngoni made of calabash with more strings
link_attribute_type_list.name: kamale ngɔni

link_attribute_type_list.description: six holed fipple flute used traditionally in pastoral settings like its cousin the spilåpipa.
link_attribute_type_list.name: härjedalspipa

link_attribute_type_list.description: ancient central asian/persian string instrument ancestor of the iranian oud. it was an important instrument in pre-islamic iran and persia. the current "Persian barbat" is more similar to the oud
link_attribute_type_list.name: barbat

link_attribute_type_list.description: a relative of the shawm, it was introduced to central and south-america by the spanish in the sixteenth and seventeenth century.
link_attribute_type_list.name: chirimía

link_attribute_type_list.description: much like the pipe and tabor, the chirimía and drum is a South American cousin.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: pair of double-reed and drum. from South America.
link_attribute_type_list.name: chirimía and drum

link_attribute_type_list.description: Invented in 1823, it had a body like a medieval fiddle, but had frets and tuning like the guitar.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: bass viol with guitar frets and tuning
link_attribute_type_list.name: arpeggione

link_attribute_type_list.description: smallest member of the Serbo-Croatian tamburica orchestra, it is usually the lead or "prim" instrument. it can either have one double string and three single strings (prim/bisernica) or two double strings and two single strings (bisernica)
link_attribute_type_list.name: bisernica

link_attribute_type_list.description: second smallest member of the Serbo-Croatian tamburica orchestra, there are often two, first and second bass. it can either have three double strings or two double strings and three single strings
link_attribute_type_list.name: brač

link_attribute_type_list.description: chord rhythm member of the Serbo-Croatian tamburica orchestra, it is guitar like. it has one double string D and three single strings
link_attribute_type_list.name: bugarija

link_attribute_type_list.description: counterpoint member of the Serbo-Coatian tamburica orchestra, it is guitar like like the bugarija. it can either have two double strings and two single strings (čelović) or now four single strings are more common. (čelo/čelović/) the čelović is a different pitch than the čelo
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: čelović or csello, counter serbo-croatian plucked stringinstrument
link_attribute_type_list.name: čelo

link_attribute_type_list.description: Bass member of the Serbo-Croatian tamburica orchestra, it is contrabass like. it has four thick metal strings
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: begeš, large bass serbo-croatian plucked stringinstrument
link_attribute_type_list.name: berda

link_attribute_type_list.description: Usually accompanying sitar on ragas, the fretless instrument comes in different sizes: largest; male-voice, smaller; female-voice and smallest; the instrument-accompanying "tanpuri"
link_attribute_type_list.name: tanpura

link_attribute_type_list.description: also known as dangubica or kuterevka, it is similar to the bisernica and the ancestor of the entire tamburica ensemble. this plucked long-necked lute has two double strings and is played solo.
link_attribute_type_list.name: samica

link_attribute_type_list.description: reinvented several times in different lands, it combines banjo body and neck of mandolin, it is tuned like it and the violin.
link_attribute_type_list.name: banjolin

link_attribute_type_list.description: small fretted hybrid instrument with a banjo body and a ukulele neck, popular in the 1920-30
link_attribute_type_list.name: banjo-ukulele

link_attribute_type_list.description: guitar-bouzouki hybrid with guitar body and 4 pairs of strings like the bouzouki, developed separately several different luthiers, among them Stefan Sobell (bouzar) and Davy Stuart (gouzouki)
link_attribute_type_list.name: bouzar / gouzouki

link_attribute_type_list.description: Inspired by the violin in 1920, Hellenic luthiers combined parts of the old Cretan lyra with the violin.
link_attribute_type_list.name: viololyra

link_attribute_type_list.description: Invented in 1966 by vox, and in 1969 by Bob Murrell, it is an electric guitar with electronic organ wiring and knobs added.
link_attribute_type_list.name: guitorgan

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Unison course tuned 12 stringed electric guitar body with mandolin neck, it is tuned an octave higher than a conventional guitar, thus having the tonal range of the mandolin.
Not to be confused with flat-backed mandolin which is also sold as "mando-guitar"
link_attribute_type_list.name: mandoguitar

link_attribute_type_list.description: Unisonoric concertina with hexagonal sides and concertina reeds.
link_attribute_type_list.name: English concertina

link_attribute_type_list.description: More usually square than hexagonal, with long plate reeds
link_attribute_type_list.name: German concertina

link_attribute_type_list.description: Bisonoric and button layout like the German, reeds and hexagonal ends like the english, this concertina was created to rival the popular German variant imported into England.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Historical hybrid between English and German style concertinas
link_attribute_type_list.name: Anglo concertina

link_attribute_type_list.description: wood accordina with piano keys invented in Italy
link_attribute_type_list.name: vibrandoneon

link_attribute_type_list.description: Like a piano, but here keyed hammers hit tuning forks.
link_attribute_type_list.name: dulcitone

link_attribute_type_list.description: portable piano with shoulder strap invented in 1795, it is a descendant of the Baroque Bauchladenspinett and a sort of early precursor of the keytar
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: late 18th century portable piano with shoulder strap
link_attribute_type_list.name: orphica

link_attribute_type_list.description: while today it is obscure, it was developed and improved for 400 years, the French flageolet has 4 holes in front and two thumb holes in the back
instrument_aliases: フレンチ・フラジオレット
link_attribute_type_list.name: flageolet

link_attribute_type_list.description: working to improve on its design, the English flageolet had 6 finger holes in front and one thumb hole in the back.
instrument_aliases: イングリッシュ・フラジオレット
link_attribute_type_list.name: English flageolet

link_attribute_type_list.description: Small Catalan flageolet used in the cobla ensemble, unlike other tabor-pipes it is also played with both hands.
link_attribute_type_list.name: flabiol

link_attribute_type_list.description: introduced in 1938, it was a popular American educational instrument.
link_attribute_type_list.name: Tonette

link_attribute_type_list.description: Diatonic button accordion used in traditional Basque ensemble
link_attribute_type_list.name: trikiti

link_attribute_type_list.description: in the form of a piano accordion, the keys control the output, pitch and sound, bellows control only volume. Sold by Hohner since 1952, it has gone through many modernisations through the years.
link_attribute_type_list.name: elektronium

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in gagaku, this traditional bamboo flute has six holes.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kagurabue

link_attribute_type_list.description: its origins can be sourced almost 5000 years ago, this Iraqi string instrument is the forefather of several instruments around the world. Its name means coconut or acorn, while originally made of walnut, it is today of coconut and fish-skin or heart-membrane.
link_attribute_type_list.name: djoza

link_attribute_type_list.description: made of clay with two layers of goat skin, it is used as a rhythmic accompaniment to shehnai players and is one of the instruments of the Baul community.
link_attribute_type_list.name: duggi

link_attribute_type_list.description: Very long metal bar fitted with strings and electric pickups, it makes a deep ominous booming sound and is often used in score.
link_attribute_type_list.name: Blaster Beam

link_attribute_type_list.description: Sound chip from various video game consoles, computers and similar, used for music, chiefly in bitunes and chiptunes
instrument_aliases: เครื่องเล่นวิดีโอเกม
ゲームボーイアドバンス
セガ・マスターシステム
ファミリーコンピュータ
link_attribute_type_list.name: game console sound chip

link_attribute_type_list.description: Four-stringed banjo-like lute from West Africa, traditionally played by griots. Made of hollowed out wood with an animal hide as membrane.
link_attribute_type_list.name: jeli ngɔni

link_attribute_type_list.description: Fipple flute that became a symbol for the Basque folk revival.
link_attribute_type_list.name: txistu

link_attribute_type_list.description: nose flute made of gourd with it two holes, while it is tiny, it has a huge sound
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro tiny gourd nose flute
link_attribute_type_list.name: kōauau ponga ihu

link_attribute_type_list.description: Gourd (hue) shakers with seeds intact
link_attribute_type_list.name: hue puruwai

link_attribute_type_list.description: large gourd (hue) with no finger-holes, it is blown like a jug and has a booming bass sound.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro large bass gourd
link_attribute_type_list.name: hue puruhau

link_attribute_type_list.description: thin quiet mouth harp like instrument made of wood or bone.
link_attribute_type_list.name: rōria

link_attribute_type_list.description: also just "kū", a mouthbow with a single string, struck with a light implement of bone or wood.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro single string bow
link_attribute_type_list.name: te kū

link_attribute_type_list.description: A pūtātara with a long wooden mouth piece, it has a deeper timbre than the pūtātara
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro long necked conch trumpet
link_attribute_type_list.name: pūpakapaka

link_attribute_type_list.description: Whirring, spinning disks of many shapes, sizes and materials. When blown on, they also create a well of different humming sounds.
link_attribute_type_list.name: porotiti

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of resonating stone, wood or bone with a striker of stone, bone or hardwood, this ancient instrument was used for rhythm during chanting.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro ancient struck idiophone
link_attribute_type_list.name: tumutumu

link_attribute_type_list.description: Logs or thick planks up to 9 meters long used as signalling drums, as gongs for both war and peace. Even living hollowed trees have been used as pahū.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro large signalling drum
link_attribute_type_list.name: pahū

link_attribute_type_list.description: Castanets of wood, bone, bivalve shells or even flax leaf.
link_attribute_type_list.name: tōkere

link_attribute_type_list.description: Flax leaf balls filled with seeds, swung around to create rhythmic and visual patterns. An important element in the dance also known as poi.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Māori taonga pūoro flax leaf balls used in dancing
link_attribute_type_list.name: poi

link_attribute_type_list.description: Two rods, one is held in the mouth, which works as a resonance chamber while chanting and singing, the other is used for tapping and scraping, creating rhythm.
link_attribute_type_list.name: pākuru

link_attribute_type_list.description: This indicates the linked entity translated something, rather than being the original writer.
link_attribute_type_list.name: translator

link_attribute_type_list.description: Indicates a sibling with whom the artist has only one parent in common
link_attribute_type_list.name: half

link_attribute_type_list.description: For indicating the step-spouse of the artist's parent, or the sibling relationship with the children of such a spouse
link_attribute_type_list.name: step

link_attribute_type_list.description: Medieval or possibly older, it was historically made of goat-horns or, less commonly, clay. With only a few holes and a single octave range it was used pastorally. Modern revival variants are more complicated and are usually made of cattle-horn.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gemshorn

link_attribute_type_list.description: Derived from the tenor shawm, it was independently developed in Catalonia by oboe player Andreu Turon in the 19th century. Made of ginjoler wood with a metal cone, it is used in the traditional Cobla and Sardana music.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Catalonian double reed descendant of shawm
link_attribute_type_list.name: tenora

link_attribute_type_list.description: Traditional Catalan double reed of medieval origin. Used for cobla music in the 19th century, it mostly disappeared: current tarotas are a modern re-invention.
link_attribute_type_list.name: tarota

link_attribute_type_list.description: Catalan double-reed, created in the 19th Century by adding valves to the tarota. Used principally for cobla music.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Catalan valved shawm used in cobla
link_attribute_type_list.name: tible

link_attribute_type_list.description: National symbol of Estonia, related to Finnish kantele. While traditionally a small instrument with 5 to 7 strings, some newer versions are much bigger, with up to 50 strings.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kannel

link_attribute_type_list.description: Invented (or atleast made popular by) Blue Man Group, it is made of alternating length PVC pipes and hit with sticks or flaps. It is popular with street musicians.
link_attribute_type_list.name: tubulum

link_attribute_type_list.description: Pipes of bamboo with goat or calfskin membranes, the pitches depend largely on tube-lenght, it is the ancestor to the octoban. Modern variants are usually made of wood, plastic or metal with plastic membranes.
link_attribute_type_list.name: boobam

link_attribute_type_list.description: Small but long tom-toms set in groups of four or eight, they are a common addition in drumsets. Originally made of fibreglass, today also acrylic, aluminium or wood is used, and homemade ones of f.ex PVC are not uncommon.
link_attribute_type_list.name: octoban

link_attribute_type_list.description: Played using circular breathing, it is made of twin bamboo reeds with five to six holes. From the middle-east, it is used in traditional music and as a accompaniment to belly dancing and dabke.
link_attribute_type_list.name: mijwiz

link_attribute_type_list.description: Use this to mark acts of an opera (as opposed to other parts that are not acts)
link_attribute_type_list.name: act

link_attribute_type_list.description: Use this when the parent work consists of several parts which are generally meant to be performed one after another in a particular order (rather than in any order, or multiple times, or in arbitrary selections rather than as a whole).
link_attribute_type_list.name: movement

link_attribute_type_list.description: Use this for operas and similar works that are separated in numbers, to specify what parts are numbers (as opposed to spoken dialog or other non-numbered parts).
link_attribute_type_list.name: number

link_attribute_type_list.description: Use this to indicate the parent work is not intended to be performed as a whole, but is just a grouping of pieces or works that can be performed individually (collections of songs, several sonatas or concertos published as a set, books of piano pieces, etc.)
link_attribute_type_list.name: part of collection

link_attribute_type_list.description: plastic horn often used at football matches in South Africa
link_attribute_type_list.name: vuvuzela

link_attribute_type_list.description: wooden flute of the simple-system type, popular in Irish folk music
link_attribute_type_list.name: Irish flute

link_attribute_type_list.description: more accurately a virginals, its strings were arranged in a pattern creating an oval shape, allowing for a stronger, more compact configuration. 2 examples survive today.
link_attribute_type_list.name: oval spinet

link_attribute_type_list.description: Larger bass drum also known as thunder or pope smasher, it has a double goat skin membrane and is hit with a padded mallet. Important in the Jamaican Burru music and Rastafari movement, it is part of the Nyabinghi/Akete drums.
link_attribute_type_list.name: baandu

link_attribute_type_list.description: Middle size/pitch drum, it has a slack goat skin membrane and it is hit with the palms. Important in the Jamaican Burru music and Rastafari movement, it is part of the Nyabinghi/Akete drums.
link_attribute_type_list.name: fundeh

link_attribute_type_list.description: Also known as kette or keteh, it is the smallest size/pitch drum. It has a tight goat skin membrane and it is struck with the fingertips. Important in the Jamaican Burru music and Rastafari movement, it is part of the Nyabinghi/Akete drums.
link_attribute_type_list.name: repeater

link_attribute_type_list.description: Related to bowed instrument rebab, it is played in many countries, from India to Afghanistan. It's made of wood and covered with a membrane and has 3 melody strings, 3 drone strings and 11/12 resonance strings.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Ancient Afghan plucked lute member of the bowed rebab family
link_attribute_type_list.name: rubab

link_attribute_type_list.description: Originally introduced to Mongolia from China, it has 13 double wire-strings and a black lacquered wooden soundboard. Traditionally it was only played by townspeople.
link_attribute_type_list.name: yoochin

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used to teach children how to play, it is a simplified zither shaped like a trapezoid.
link_attribute_type_list.name: lap harp

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of thick aged bamboo, this notched end-blown flute may have had a buzzing membrane.
link_attribute_type_list.name: tungso

link_attribute_type_list.description: Hunting horn carved out of ivory, it was widely used in Medieval wars to alarm or increase soldiers' morale.
link_attribute_type_list.name: olifant

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of tubing with holes that may have everything from none to two reeds, water instead of air comes out of the holes. Sound is produced by covering the holes with fingers, altering the flow of water through the holes.
link_attribute_type_list.name: hydraulophone

link_attribute_type_list.description: Shaped like an eighth-note with a cute face, it is played with one hand fingering the neck and the other squeezing the head to create wavering sound.
link_attribute_type_list.name: Otamatone

link_attribute_type_list.description: From atleast 600 BC, it is made of wood to resemble a tiger with its spine made into notches. Notches are scraped to produce sound, it was used in ensemble with zhu.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: wooden tiger scraped with bamboo whisk
link_attribute_type_list.name: yu

link_attribute_type_list.description: Sacred cylindrical goat or cattle rawhide drum, traditionally it was held with straps or placed on a stand and played horizontally while walking to battle, on special state occasions and religious ceremonies.
link_attribute_type_list.name: junjung

link_attribute_type_list.description: Originating from 13th century Latvia, it is made of wood and have today nine to eleven, up to thirty-three strings tuned diatonically. Played horizontally on lap or table by plucking while muting chosen strings. Became in the 70's and 80's an important part of the folklore movement.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kokle

link_attribute_type_list.description: Effectively a small electric guitar-piano hybrid, it is played by tapping the multiple isometric arranged strings that are stretched over a long flat board.
link_attribute_type_list.name: harpejji

link_attribute_type_list.description: Invented by European luthiers in the first half of the 18th century, it had 6 to 7 single or double courses of gut strings and a long neck with either sharp or shallow angled pegbox.
link_attribute_type_list.name: mandora / gallichon

link_attribute_type_list.description: Indicates that the relationship is optional - doesn't always apply.
link_attribute_type_list.name: optional

link_attribute_type_list.description: Originating 1590 or earlier and made of wood or ivory, it had nine parallel cylindrical bores joined up at the top and bottom creating a continuous bore like a curled up snake. Its reed is attached to a center pirouette.
link_attribute_type_list.name: renaissance rackett

link_attribute_type_list.description: Improved by Johann Christoph Denner sometime around 1730, its "coiled snake" bore now is conical, in addition it is played through a removable metal curled mouth piece.
link_attribute_type_list.name: baroque rackett

link_attribute_type_list.description: New Violin Family of eight proportionally-sized violins developed mainly by Carleen Hutchins, each based directly on the standard violin and its acoustic properties, thus their sound is at a more homogeneous pitch "near the two middle open strings."
instrument_aliases: バイオリンオクテット
ヴァイオリンオクテット
ヴァイオリン・オクテット
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: New modern streamlined family of violins
link_attribute_type_list.name: violin octet

link_attribute_type_list.description: Please use Viola da Gamba with credit "viol" for generic / unspecified viol credits, and not this one. Developed in the 15th century from vihuelists starting to play with bows, viols have frets, flat backs, sloped shoulders, c-holes and are tuned in fourths.
link_attribute_type_list.name: viol family

link_attribute_type_list.description: Sixteenth century predecessor of the violoncello, it was fretless with f-holes and had at first three, then later four, strings tuned in fifths.
link_attribute_type_list.name: bass violin

link_attribute_type_list.description: Based on the polyrhythm of the Afrikan kora, kalimba and Japanese koto, it is electro-acoustic with a stainless steel frame with 24 nylon strings and an integral piezoelectric sensor.
link_attribute_type_list.name: Gravikord

link_attribute_type_list.description: Meane or mean is a young male singer with a voice lower than a treble
link_attribute_type_list.name: meane vocals

link_attribute_type_list.description: Traditionally used in samba and maracatu, it originates from the European military battalion. It is made of a 30-35 cm diameter metal barrel strung with a synthetic material, upon which a steel wire or guitar string is strung across.
link_attribute_type_list.name: caixa

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of wood, bone or metal, this ancient instrument is the forerunner to many other struck idiophones
link_attribute_type_list.name: clapper

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in traditional kabuki and bunraku theatre, it consists of two hardwood pieces tied together with ornamental cord.
link_attribute_type_list.name: hyoshigi

link_attribute_type_list.description: Counter-trapezoid shaped Mongolian fiddle, it is the ancestor of the Morin khuur
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ancient predecessor of the Morin khuur
link_attribute_type_list.name: chuurqin

link_attribute_type_list.description: Originating from Henan province, this fretless huqin has the accustomary two strings, but is made of wood.
link_attribute_type_list.name: zhuihu

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chamber ensemble of four players (two violins, one viola, one cello)
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ensemble of 1st violin, 2nd violin, viola and cello
link_attribute_type_list.name: string quartet

link_attribute_type_list.description: instruments used in the traditional music of the Basque people
link_attribute_type_list.name: traditional basque ensemble

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chamber ensemble of three players (one piano, one violin, one cello)
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ensemble of violin, cello and piano
link_attribute_type_list.name: piano trio

link_attribute_type_list.description: Popular in the fifteenth century, it was a combination of a pipe organ and harpsichord, often with two keyboards, one for the strings and one for the pipes mounted underneath.
link_attribute_type_list.name: claviorganum

link_attribute_type_list.description: Ancestor of the modern trumpet, it is valveless with a mouthpiece and at-least one coil, it has an origin in military use.
instrument_aliases: ナチュラル トランペット
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Brass valveless ancestor of the modern trumpet
link_attribute_type_list.name: natural trumpet

link_attribute_type_list.description: Family of the brass instrument trumpet
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Family of the brass instrument trumpet
link_attribute_type_list.name: trumpet family

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chamber ensemble of three players (one violin, one viola, one cello)
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ensemble of violin, viola and cello
link_attribute_type_list.name: string trio

link_attribute_type_list.description: Most common viol member today, it was the bass-range viol in the original consort.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Bass member of the viol family
link_attribute_type_list.name: bass viol

link_attribute_type_list.description: Predecessor of the modern classical guitar, it had gut strings and even gut frets. First described in 1555, it surpassed the Renaissance lute's popularity.
link_attribute_type_list.name: baroque guitar

link_attribute_type_list.description: Popular in the renaissance, it was small and portable with two bellows and brass reeds.
link_attribute_type_list.name: regal

link_attribute_type_list.description: Use this to mark that a tour was for the anniversary (rather than the launch) of a release group.
link_attribute_type_list.name: anniversary

link_attribute_type_list.description: Indicates how many of a specific instrument an ensemble usually includes.
link_attribute_type_list.name: amount

link_attribute_type_list.description: Originating in Mewati community of Rahajastan, India, it is traditionally made of gourd with top and bottom cut of with a goat hide membrane nailed to one opening and a gut string which ends in a bamboo piece attached to the membrane. The instrument is held in the armpit and played by plucking the string while pulling the it.
link_attribute_type_list.name: bhapang

link_attribute_type_list.description: The treble, descant or soprano member of the viol family
instrument_aliases: ドゥシュ で ヴィオラ
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Treble/soprano member of the viol family
link_attribute_type_list.name: treble viol

link_attribute_type_list.description: Tenor member of the viol family
instrument_aliases: テノール ヴィオラ
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Tenor member of the viol family
link_attribute_type_list.name: tenor viol

link_attribute_type_list.description: Alt, alto or contralto member of the viol family. Not to be confused with the Viola of the violin family, known as "Alto" in French.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Alto member of the viol family
link_attribute_type_list.name: alto viol

link_attribute_type_list.description: Pardessus or sopranino member of the viol family
instrument_aliases: パルドゥシュ で ヴィオラ
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Smallest member of the viol family
link_attribute_type_list.name: pardessus de viole

link_attribute_type_list.description: Small bass viol, specialised for intricate polyphonics, it had a flatter bridge than the other bass viols.
link_attribute_type_list.name: lyra viol

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in folk music and educationally in Korean schools, it is a notched, vertically end-blown bamboo flute which, like its bigger cousin the Tungso, was derived from the Chinese xiao in the 19th century.
link_attribute_type_list.name: danso

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of palmyra leaves that act as a resonator and a bamboo-tube stuck with wooden pegs where the plucked strings are attached, this traditional Indonesian instrument has an important place in folk history. It is still being used and modernised.
link_attribute_type_list.name: sasando

link_attribute_type_list.description: Its name variously used to describe the serpentone, the ophicleide and the bombardon, the modern cimbasso was developed by instrument making firm Pelitti under instruction guidance by Verdi; it had a forward facing bell and 3 to 6 valves. The modern instrument has a characteristic bend.
link_attribute_type_list.name: cimbasso

link_attribute_type_list.description: Plucked idiophones have spikes, nails or flexible tongues ("lamellae") that are plucked in various ways to produce sound.
link_attribute_type_list.name: plucked idiophone

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Having 4 metal strings, it is carved from a single piece of wood into a particular shape similar to the Sarinda, it has 3 soundholes, two on each side of the short neck and one under the bridge which is membrane-covered.
Not to be confused with the Uyghur spike fiddle ghijak.
link_attribute_type_list.name: ghaychak

link_attribute_type_list.description: Not to be confused with the Persian lute ghaychak.
link_attribute_type_list.name: ghijak

link_attribute_type_list.description: This attribute indicates a member the band is named after and without which it wouldn't exist (e.g. Miles Davis -> Miles Davis Sextet)
link_attribute_type_list.name: eponymous

link_attribute_type_list.description: Located in the Luray Caverns of Virginia USA, it is made of an electric console wired to mallets striking thirty-seven stalactites producing tones. This is a singular specific instrument, not an instrument type.
link_attribute_type_list.name: The Great Stalacpipe Organ

link_attribute_type_list.description: Constructed as the uttermost largest member of the violin family, it is up towards 4 meters and has three strings operated by levers and pedals.
link_attribute_type_list.name: octobass

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of goatskin covered dried gourd, it has a single gut string bowed with a horsehair bow.
link_attribute_type_list.name: njarka

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of metal with many small notches scraped with a brush or hair pick, it is used in Merengue típico.
link_attribute_type_list.name: güira

link_attribute_type_list.description: Deriving from the classical guitar, it has many modifications and playing techniques to create a more percussive sound. Originally having wooden tuning pegs like lutes and violins, it has a thinner, lighter build with "golpeadores" or tapping-plates and less sustain than the classical guitar.
link_attribute_type_list.name: flamenco guitar

link_attribute_type_list.description: Developed chiefly as a folk instrument for accompanying singing and dancing, its front has a intricately carved soundhole and slants towards the bottom where the 5 double (sometimes triple courses) of thin steel strings are fastened with pins. The back can be rounded or flat.
link_attribute_type_list.name: chitarra battente

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in Spanish classic and popular music during the late 19th and early 20th century, it together with bandurria and laúd carried the melody in rondas and rondallas. It had six pairs of strings, tuned a fourth lower than bandurria, It fell out of use when contralto bandurria became common.
link_attribute_type_list.name: octavilla

link_attribute_type_list.description: Originating with the Spanish influence on Filipino culture, it has 14 strings and a short neck with 16 to 20 frets. It is played with its close relative, the laúd.
link_attribute_type_list.name: octavina

link_attribute_type_list.description: ensemble of four saxophones, generally SATBar
instrument_aliases: サクソフォーン四重奏
link_attribute_type_list.name: saxophone quartet

link_attribute_type_list.description:
One of the two subfamilies of Indian chordophones, the South Indian gourd lute used in classical Carnatic music has a single gourd music-box (sometimes a secondary small resonator gourd) with an often hollow neck.
Commonly called just "veena" or "vina", the representative is Saraswati veena, and credits to just "veena" are usually that.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Family of ancient Indian lute-like chordophones
link_attribute_type_list.name: veena

link_attribute_type_list.description: zither with a hollow or vaulted body, it can have several resonators
link_attribute_type_list.name: tube zither

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Used in India during the medieval period, it had a stick-zither body with 3 strings
For the the long necked lute called "tritantri veena" in the 19 century, see "sitar"
link_attribute_type_list.name: tritantri veena

link_attribute_type_list.description: Effectively a bass sitar, it has 4 main, 3-4 chikari(drone) and 10-11 resonance strings strung over a wide wooden neck. Made of flat-cut gourd with a possible secondary resonator (tumba) it was invented in 1825 by sitar-player Ghulam Mohammed who wanted a deeper sound.
link_attribute_type_list.name: surbahar

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Flat-cut gourd sitar with 16 frets, 2 steel & 3 brass strings. In the 1820s it was used by Ghulam Mohammed to develop the surbahar.

Mistakenly re-named "Kachapi vina" in the 19th century by Tagore, that instrument was actually an ancient unrelated short-necked pear-shaped lute.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kachva sitar

link_attribute_type_list.description: Often used in group musical education and by street musicians, it consists of individual colourful hollow plastic tubes of various widths that are struck on various surfaces to produce sound, tubes can also be arranged and hit by mallets.
link_attribute_type_list.name: boomwhacker

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of various materials, clappers are struck together to keep rhythm and time for use in ceremonies and rituals and to accompany dancing and singing.
link_attribute_type_list.name: krap

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in folk music, bamboo is split into a pair of 40 cm long sticks which are hit together.
link_attribute_type_list.name: krap khū

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in royal ceremonies, played by striking against the palm, it is made of several pieces of wood, ivory and sheets of brass between two larger pieces of wood tied together by a string at one end.
link_attribute_type_list.name: krap phuang

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in the chant, sēphā, a pair of squared bevelled rosewood sticks around 21 cm long and 3-4 cm thick.
link_attribute_type_list.name: krap sēphā

link_attribute_type_list.description: Shaken idiophone or rattle. Sound is produced by holding or containing usually small concussing parts.
link_attribute_type_list.name: shaken idiophone

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used by Australian Aboriginals to maintain rhythm in chants and traditionally to accompany didgeridoo. There are also Boomerang clapsticks.
link_attribute_type_list.name: clapstick

link_attribute_type_list.description: Important in Yue, Kunkqu and Peking opera, it consists of the drum bangu and the clapper paiban. Drum is played by one hand and clapper with the other.
link_attribute_type_list.name: guban

link_attribute_type_list.description: Made of sandalwood or bamboo, 2 blocks are tied together with a string, a third with a ribbon. Part of the drum and clapper ensemble guban, but confusingly, the clapper alone can also be called guban.
link_attribute_type_list.name: paiban

link_attribute_type_list.description: Generally consists of two completely equal or somewhat unsymmetrical sticks, plates, shells or similar hit together.
link_attribute_type_list.name: concussion idiophone

link_attribute_type_list.description: Generally consists of one to very many plates, sticks, or other objects hit with (or against) various types of mallets.
link_attribute_type_list.name: percussion idiophone

link_attribute_type_list.description: Scraper made of bones tied together with rope scraped with a conch-shell, in Catalonia it's scraped with a castanet. Ginebra is a variant made of cane.
link_attribute_type_list.name: arrabel

link_attribute_type_list.description: Uses in Javanese gamelan and Balinese wayang, these range from 12 to 14 tuned bronze keys, all suspended over metal or bamboo resonators.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gendèr

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in Javanese gamelan, the 6(slendro)-7(pelog) bronze keys beat by a disk mallet(tabuh) are suspended above individual tube metal/bamboo resonators set in a 75 cm long wooden frame.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Deepest pitch single octave metallophone used in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: slenthem

link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Arrangement of struck tuned stone bars.
link_attribute_type_list.name: lithophone

link_attribute_type_list.description: Specifies the subject that was taught by a teacher in an institution and/or to a student.
link_attribute_type_list.name: subject

link_attribute_type_list.description: Specifies the level of studies that a student studied towards in an educational institution.
link_attribute_type_list.name: level of studies

link_attribute_type_list.description: Unlike the other saron, its six-seven keys are not smooth, but bevelled or bossed, like the gendèr and the slenthem, which has largely replaced it in the modernised gamelan.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Obsolete deepest saron-family member used in Sundanese gamelan.
link_attribute_type_list.name: slentho

link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ensemble of piano, violin, viola and cello
link_attribute_type_list.name: piano quartet

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Used traditionally by the Minang people of West Sumatra for love songs, in shamanic rites and for capturing tigers, this open end-blown bamboo flute can be decorated to resemble tiger-stripes. Played with a circular breathing technique, it is 75 cm long with 3 to 6 holes depending on subtype.
  • saluang darek "inland" 4 holes
  • saluang pauh/padang 6 holes
  • saluang sungai pagu 3 holes
link_attribute_type_list.name: saluang

link_attribute_type_list.description: Even lower pitched than the pemade , it also has 10 bronze keys suspended over tuned bamboo resonators that the player hits with a small hammer, often in a theatrical manner. It is the leader of the pokok melody.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Deep pitch gangsa used in Balinese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: ugal

link_attribute_type_list.description: Extra large double-headed water buffalo hide barrel drum. It is suspended from a rack and beat with mallet, it is used in central Javanese gamelan and for religious signalling purposes in mosques.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Very large suspended Javanese barrel drum
link_attribute_type_list.name: bedug

link_attribute_type_list.description: Consisting of bossed bronze kettle-like gongs arranged in elaborately decorated wooden frames, these are hit with tabuh beaters.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Family of bronze gong-chime sets used in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: bonang

link_attribute_type_list.description: One to two octaves below panerus, it plays elaborate melodies and is one of the most important instruments in the ensemble.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Medium pitched gong-chime set in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: bonang barung

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used to play fast melodies, it is the highest pitched member of the bonang family of gong-chimes
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Highest pitched gong-chime set in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: bonang panerus

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chiefly used in Yogyvanese gamelan, the largest and deepest pitched member of the bonang family of gong-chimes
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Lowest pitched gong-chime set in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: bonang panembung

link_attribute_type_list.description: This specifies the credited task(s) for a relationship that can be credited in many different ways, such as "producer", or one that is otherwise generic, such as "miscellaneous support".
link_attribute_type_list.name: task

link_attribute_type_list.description: Whistling (with mouth, not a whistle)
link_attribute_type_list.name: whistling

link_attribute_type_list.description: Set of 4 to 12 small metal gongs suspended in a string carriage, played by two to four players.
link_attribute_type_list.name: reyong

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Made of multiple vertical hung bamboo tubes with segments cut out to create different pitch. They can be played either hanging suspended or on a bamboo frame like a xylophone.
For the balinese metallophone sometimes known as chalung or calung, see jublag
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Bamboo xylophone used in Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: calung

link_attribute_type_list.description: Highest pitched gangsa, it has 10 brass or bronze keys suspended over short resonating bamboo pipes set in an heavily ornate case.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: High pitch gangsa used in Balinese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kantilan

link_attribute_type_list.description: Second highest pitched gangsa, it is deeper than the kantil but also has 10 brass or bronze keys suspended over short resonating pipes set in an heavily ornate case.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Lower pitch gangsa used in Balinese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: pemade

link_attribute_type_list.description: Consists of up to 21 hardwood bars suspended by pins and rope over an oft ornate wooden trapeze shaped box, the two springy beaters are made of buffalo horn.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gambang

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in both gamelan and Wayang puppet theatre, it consists of two small cymbals sets; the bottom pair affixed in a highly decorated rancak rack, their lose counterparts attached together by string.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kecer

link_attribute_type_list.description: Largest and lowest pitched of the gangsa, it has 5 to 7 large metal bars suspended over long bamboo resonators
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Deepest pitch gangsa used in Balinese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: jegogan

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Second lowest pitched of the gangsa, it has 4 to 7 bronze keys suspended over long bamboo resonators
For the sundanese bamboo xylophone also used in gamelan, see calung
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Second deepest pitch gangsa used in Balinese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: jublag

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chiefly used in Southeast Asian music, these consist of from only 1 to several dozens of metal (often bronze) "kettles", set in a wide variety of different supports and played with mallets.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gong-chime

link_attribute_type_list.description: Singular bronze gong-chime kettle set on a decorated jackfruit-wood box stand. Used as time or tempo keeper, it is struck on the boss with panggul beater, which provides reference point in the pokok.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Singular metal timekeeping gong-chime used in Balinese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kempli

link_attribute_type_list.description: Free reeds with bellows attached, often in a square shape, thence called squeezebox
link_attribute_type_list.name: bellowed reed

link_attribute_type_list.description: Set of 6 to 10 smaller hanging pitched bronze gongs with a protruding centre knob struck with tabuh beaters. Higher pitched than the gong ageng, it is often hung together with it.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kempul

link_attribute_type_list.description: Related to bonang, it consists of 3 decorated rancak frames that each hold 2 large arched(jaler) bronze gong-chimes hit by heavy tabuh beater(s).
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Large high-pitch gong-chime used in Indonesian Gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kenong

link_attribute_type_list.description: Always used with kempyang, it consists of a single bronze kettle-gong, pitched deeper than kempyangs'. Flat top bossed gong is sat in a highly decorated rancak frame, beaten with tabuh beaters by the same person.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Small boxed gong-chime used in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kethuk

link_attribute_type_list.description: Always used with ketuk, it consists of two bronze kettle-gongs, pitched a note apart. Ached top bossed gongs are sat in the highly decorated rancak frame, beaten with tabuh beaters by the same person.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Small boxed double gong-chime used in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kempyang

link_attribute_type_list.description: Bent into a ladle or banana like shape, it is a pair of bronze slit-drums hit with tabuh beaters.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Banana shaped bronze slit-drum used in javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kemanak

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Used in many Southeast Asian countries, the body is made of woods like jackfruit or coconut, the heads of buffalo- and goat-hide.
There are 2 main groups:
  • The Java/Sunda set of 3 to 4 curve sided drums with heads of different sizes.
  • The Bali set of two pairs of straight sided drums, both heads same size.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Family of two sided laced drums used in various gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendang

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Largest or "ageng" of the kendang, it is still smaller than the bedug.
In Sunda it is known as kendhang gedé.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Largest conical kendang used in both Javanese/Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendhang gendhing

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Smallest of the kendang, it is has a special two-instrument use with the gendhing.
In Sunda it is known as kendhang katipung
instrument_aliases: クンダン・クティプン
クンダン・クティポン
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Smallest conical kendang used in both Javanese/Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendhang ketipung

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Middle sized kendang, it is used in lively and complex rhythms.
In Sunda it is known as kendhang ciblon
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Middle conical kendang used in both Javanese/Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendhang batangan

link_attribute_type_list.description: Middle sized kendang, it was traditionally used for the wayang dance.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Middle conical kendang used in javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendhang wayangan

link_attribute_type_list.description: The smaller and higher pitched of the Bali kendang, it is the follower "male" drum.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: High pitched cylindrical kendang used in Bali gamelan.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendang lanang

link_attribute_type_list.description: The larger and deeper pitched of the Bali kendang, it is the leader "female" drum.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Low pitched cylindrical kendang used in Bali gamelan.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendang wadon

link_attribute_type_list.description: Played in ensemble with kulanter, it is set at an angle and played with hands or sticks. it has the designation of "mother"
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: King-Size truncated cone kendang used in Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendhang indung

link_attribute_type_list.description: Always used together with the indung, dual child drums are divided into the katipung ("besar" or larger) and the kutiplak ("kecil" or smaller).
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Paired truncated cone kendang used in Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kendhang kulanter

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in wayang puppetry, it is made of 1 to 4 iron (sometimes bronze) plates hung by strings, The puppeteer uses a small mallet set in the foot to hit the plate(s).
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Metal plate percussion used in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kepyak

link_attribute_type_list.description: There are two kinds, boat shaped "mother" and "child" ones, originally made of a single piece of wood and descended from the kacapi pantun as used in epic story. And a flat box-zither made of wood used from the 1950's and in experimental music.
link_attribute_type_list.name: kacapi

link_attribute_type_list.description: Inspired by western zithers, it has 20 strings and is flatter than the other kacapis.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Box zither used in Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kacapi siter

link_attribute_type_list.description: Known as indung "mother" or parahu "boat", it has 18 brass strings each with its own pyramid shaped bridge. Shaped like a boat, its sound hole is at the bottom.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Boat shaped zither used in Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kacapi indung

link_attribute_type_list.description: Known as rincik "child" it has 15 steel strings, it has individual bridges and a sound-hole in the bottom.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Small boat shaped zither used in Sundanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: kacapi rincik

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used for simple ornamentation, it has thick small metal bars suspended above resonator rods, it is the highest pitched member of the gendèr family.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Higest pitch metallophone used in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: gendèr panerus

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used for elaborate and complex parts, it has thinner but wider metal bars suspended above resonator rods, it is the middle-pitched member of the gendèr family.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Middle pitch metallophone used in Javanese gamelan
link_attribute_type_list.name: gendèr barung

link_attribute_type_list.description: Special version of the gendèr used in Gamelan gendèr wayang performances. Tuned in Slendro, one to two pairs are played using the same hand to both play and dampen the keys.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Ten key metallophone used in Bali wayang.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gendèr wayang

link_attribute_type_list.description: Called simply "demung", it is the largest and deepest pitched of the regular saron.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Penultimate pitch saron-family member used in Sundanese gamelan.
link_attribute_type_list.name: saron demung

link_attribute_type_list.description: Often called simply "saron", it plays the basic, balungan melody
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Middle pitch saron-family member used in Sundanese gamelan.
link_attribute_type_list.name: saron barung

link_attribute_type_list.description: Called simply "panerus", it has seven bronze keys resting on a decorated rancak box. Keys are hit by tabuh mallet with head of buffalo horn.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Highest pitch saron-family member used in Sundanese gamelan.
link_attribute_type_list.name: saron panerus

link_attribute_type_list.description: Similar to panerus with which it has a different but overlapping range, it has 6 bronze keys tuned for the slendro key.
link_attribute_type_list.name: saron peking

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in the Javanese Wayang puppet theatre, it has nine large bronze keys set in a decorated frame.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Special saron used for Wayang puppetry
link_attribute_type_list.name: saron wayang

link_attribute_type_list.description: Introduced to Indonesia in the 15th century, this variant of the spike-fiddle rebab has two bowed metal strings with characteristically long tuning pegs and is made of hide-covered wood or coconut shell. It is the melody leader in gamelan and is also used in healing rituals.
link_attribute_type_list.name: Indonesian rebab

link_attribute_type_list.description:
One of the two subfamilies of Indian chordophones, the North Indian stick zither used in classical Hindustani music has two (or more) large gourd sound resonators and a stick or tube body.
Commonly called "bin", "been" or "bean", the representative is rudra veena, and credits to just "bin" are usually that.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Family of ancient Indian stick zither chordophones
link_attribute_type_list.name: bīn

link_attribute_type_list.description: Larger, with a longer neck than the kabuli rabab, it has a hide covered soundbox and wooden fingerboard with mostly gut strings, it was developed during medieval times by Tansen, who improved it and gave it metal sympathetic strings.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Medieval Indian evolution of the Afghan (kabuli) rabab
link_attribute_type_list.name: seni rebab

link_attribute_type_list.description:
For the wheel-bowed instrument also called "lira", see hurdy-gurdy
For the harp-like instrument, see lyre
For the bowed lute, see Cretan lyra
link_attribute_type_list.name: lira da braccio

link_attribute_type_list.description: Particular type of fiddle, long neck, often few strings and usually with a rather small soundbox, it has a spike at the end.
link_attribute_type_list.name: spike-fiddle

link_attribute_type_list.description: Large square spring-containing bellows operated by the feet, it was used by accordion players as bass accompaniment in early 20th century Wallonia.
link_attribute_type_list.name: footbass

link_attribute_type_list.description: Constructed in medieval Europe to be as portable as possible, small upright wooden frame with keyboard contains a row of flute-pipes fed air from hand operated bellows.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Very small bellows operated pipe organ
link_attribute_type_list.name: portative

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Not to be confused with mellophone, a brass valved instrument.
Wooden body shaped like a lute, it has a bellow operated by a handle at the bottom and buttons operating the reeds in the "neck"
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Bellowed free reed in a lute-like casing
link_attribute_type_list.name: melophone

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chamber ensemble of five players (flute, clarinet, violin, cello and piano). Originally required for Schönberg's Pierrot Lunaire, it became a fairly common combination for 20th century classical groups.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ensemble flute, clarinet, violin, cello and piano
link_attribute_type_list.name: Pierrot ensemble

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chamber ensemble where two pianists play on a different piano each.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ensemble of two pianists playing on separate pianos
link_attribute_type_list.name: piano duo

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chamber ensemble where two pianists play on the same piano at the same time.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ensemble of two pianists playing on one piano
link_attribute_type_list.name: piano four hands

link_attribute_type_list.description: From West Africa, it has multiple curved necks each with one string, sometimes played a harp and sometimes with stops like a lute.
link_attribute_type_list.name: pluriarc

link_attribute_type_list.description: Ensemble of multiple instruments of the viol family, usually including at least treble, tenor and bass viols.
link_attribute_type_list.name: viol consort

link_attribute_type_list.description: Composite chordophone, box zither where some or all strings are operated by typewriter-keys or buttons (keyboard)
link_attribute_type_list.name: keyed box zither

link_attribute_type_list.description: Similar to the Japanese taishogoto, it has an elongated teardrop shaped wooden body with button accordion like keys which plays the melody and sets chords..
link_attribute_type_list.name: akkordolia

link_attribute_type_list.description: Whole or half-pear shaped wooden body, it is partly fretted and has two string-courses; melody and drone. The name comes from Germanic words meaning bumblebee or buzzing.
link_attribute_type_list.name: hummel

link_attribute_type_list.description: Class of instruments where the body is a simple stick with one to many strings attached, may also have up to several usually gourd or wood resonators.
link_attribute_type_list.name: stick zither

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Stick zither from Rajasthan. It is made of wood with at least two large gourd resonators, the strings rest on very high frets.

Not to be confused with the 13th century tritantri-vina which evolved into the bin via a five-string stick-zither also known as "jantar".
link_attribute_type_list.name: jantar

link_attribute_type_list.description: Chamber ensemble of five players (two violins, one viola & one cello, then either a second viola, a second cello or a double bass)
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: ensemble of 2 violins, viola, cello and a fifth string instrument
link_attribute_type_list.name: string quintet

link_attribute_type_list.description: Developed from the 1960's and used mainly in the 70-80's, it was a cheaper and more portable simplification of the mellotron, emulating string ensembles. Its later hybrid development with the electronic organ created "polyphonic ensemble" synthesizers.
instrument_aliases: ストリングアンサンブル
link_attribute_type_list.name: string synthesizer

link_attribute_type_list.description: This indicates that this is a karaoke recording of the work. This is different from an instrumental recording in that it is actively meant to sing on top of, and as such the lyrics info is still relevant. It might still contain backing vocals and other sections that would not be present in a true instrumental recording.
link_attribute_type_list.name: karaoke

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in traditional Andes music, it consists of a single row of pipes.
link_attribute_type_list.name: antara

link_attribute_type_list.description: Smaller than the charango, it has 10 strings.
link_attribute_type_list.name: walaycho

link_attribute_type_list.description: An octave lower than the charango, it is the largest member of the family.
link_attribute_type_list.name: ronroco

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in folklore music of Andes, it is a descendant of European bass drum. It has a shoulder strap and two soft headed mallets used to strike the goat or llama pelt membrane.
link_attribute_type_list.name: bombo legüero

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used occasionally in the western classical orchestra and theatre, it consists of several tuned aluminium, steel or bronze plates, struck to imitate the sound of bells.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Set of tuned metal plates used in western orchestra
link_attribute_type_list.name: bell plate

link_attribute_type_list.description: Known since 1400, traditionally in military context, today it is the most widespread and famous of bagpipes.
instrument_aliases: グレート・ハイランド・バグパイプ
link_attribute_type_list.name: Great Highland bagpipe

link_attribute_type_list.description: Early predecessor to the diatonic accordion, it was similar to the German concertina and had 4 fold bellows and brass reeds.
link_attribute_type_list.name: flutina

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used in Russian, Mari and Caucasus folk music, it has two rows of buttons in a diatonic scale, as well as additional bass buttons.
link_attribute_type_list.name: garmon

link_attribute_type_list.description: Developed in Warszaw, Poland, it is used in folk music. It has two foot-operated bellows attached via metal/brass pipe; its body-bellows are only used for accents.
instrument_aliases: ペダル アコーディオン
link_attribute_type_list.name: pedal accordion

link_attribute_type_list.description: Small Bulgarian/Macedonian and Balkan goat or sheep-skin bagpipe used in folk, pastoral or other traditional settings. There are many similarly named small bagpipes in this area.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gaida

link_attribute_type_list.description: From Sanabria, it is distinct from other Spanish bagpipes, with its single drone and open fingering, it is accompanied by percussion in folk music.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gaita sanabresa

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used at-least since the 9th century, it has two to three drones with bag and pipe both decorated with fur. Used in festivals by marching players accompanied by percussion.
link_attribute_type_list.instrument_comment: Two to three drone Galician bagpipe
link_attribute_type_list.name: gaita gallega

link_attribute_type_list.description: Larger than the gallega, it has been in use since atleast the 13th century, traditionally with only one chanter and drone, it has very colourfully decorated fur.
instrument_aliases: アストリアン・パイプ
ガイタ アストリアーナ
link_attribute_type_list.name: gaita asturiana

link_attribute_type_list.description: Historically solo instrument used in traditional and ritual dances, the drones have reinforced pewter rings and the goatskin bag is covered with a colourful fabric.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gaita de boto

link_attribute_type_list.description: Czech-polish bellow-blown large goatskin bag with a 6 finger hole chanter and a long angular drone pipe that is balanced over the shoulder, the two flared pipes have inlaid metal designs and can have goat head decorations.
link_attribute_type_list.name: gajdy

link_attribute_type_list.description: With a bag made of seal's stomach, 5-6 holed chanter of juniper, pine or ash and 1-2 drones, it was attested at least from the 14th century. It survived in coastal regions until its recent revival.
link_attribute_type_list.name: torupill

link_attribute_type_list.description: This indicates the publisher subcontracted to publish a release or work in a specific territory. Use this only when you have a specific sub-publisher credit, do not guess.
link_attribute_type_list.name: sub

link_attribute_type_list.description: Used by shepherds and peoples of the southeast Black Sea region, it is droneless with parallel double chanters each with 5 fingerholes.
link_attribute_type_list.name: tulum

link_attribute_type_list.description: Developed from the veuze and often accompanying the bombard, the small high-pitched, singular octave bagpipe is used in Breton folk dancing.
link_attribute_type_list.name: biniou

link_attribute_type_list.description: Dance dance baby!
link_attribute_type_list.name: dancer

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Not to be confused with the renaissance double-reed cornamuse.
For Italian cornamusa, see bagpipe.
While also a general term for bagpipes in french, they have two telescoping drones with single reeds and a double-reed chanter.
link_attribute_type_list.name: testing a blup

link_attribute_type_list.description:
Not to be confused with the renaissance double-reed cornamuse.
For Italian cornamusa, see bagpipe.
While also a general term for bagpipes in french, they have two telescoping drones with single reeds and a double-reed chanter.
link_attribute_type_list.name: cornemuse du centre
